subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in Sustainable Design and Construction
Undergraduate degree in Environmental Engineering
Completed coursework in building decarbonization, clean energy systems, and energy modeling
Participated in research projects focused on sustainable building practices
Attended workshops on advanced simulation tools and sustainable material selection

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Title: Sustainability Specialist (Graduate Student/Research Assistant)
Focuses on clean energy integration and building decarbonization in academic projects
Responsible for energy modeling, site logistics planning, and reporting simulation results
Works on optimizing building design for energy efficiency and sustainability
Collaborates with faculty and peers on research and coursework assignments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Active in the construction and sustainable design industry
Works primarily within the academic and research sector
Engages with clean energy and building technology markets
Involved in the green building and environmental engineering sector

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Operates within a mid-to-large academic institution (graduate program)
Research group size typically ranges from 5 to 20 members
Collaborates with interdisciplinary teams across architecture, engineering, and construction management
Environment fosters innovation, research, and practical project applications

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Focused on sustainable building design and construction
Engaged in the academic research and education segment
Projects address energy optimization and decarbonization for commercial and institutional buildings
Participates in the clean energy and green technology market

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a sustainability specialist and graduate student
Started current role immediately after undergraduate graduation
Experience includes both coursework and hands-on research projects
Participated in internships prior to beginning master's program

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Early-career professional at the graduate student/entry-level specialist tier
Holds a junior/associate position within research groups
Typically supervised by professors or senior researchers
Starting to take on more independent responsibilities in projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Performs energy modeling for building projects using OpenStudio and Insight
Conducts site logistics planning and creates 2D/3D site maps using AutoCAD and FUZOR
Tracks construction schedules and project timelines in ALICE
Prepares detailed reports based on simulation and modeling results
Analyzes climate data and environmental context for optimizing site and building design

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Energy modeling and simulation: ~40% of weekly workload
Site logistics mapping and planning: ~20%
Schedule tracking and project management: ~15%
Report writing and data analysis: ~20%
Meetings and collaboration: ~5%

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in selecting modeling parameters and simulation tools
Consults with supervisors for major design decisions and project direction
Independently generates initial analysis and recommendations for energy optimization
Collaborates with peers on project teams but is responsible for assigned work segments
Can propose changes to design based on simulation outcomes, subject to approval

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Constructing 2D site maps in AutoCAD is a routine and comfortable task
Running basic energy modeling simulations in OpenStudio is straightforward
Compiling and formatting reports in Excel is familiar
Extracting and analyzing climate data for environmental reports comes easily

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Optimizing building orientation and window placement for maximum energy efficiency is complex
Integrating multiple software platforms (e.g., REVIT with OpenStudio) can be technically demanding
Interpreting simulation results when conflicting outcomes arise requires critical analysis
Selecting the best material combinations for decarbonization involves balancing trade-offs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Transcribing meeting notes and converting them into action points is repetitive
Organizing and cleaning large data sheets in Excel is data-heavy and suitable for automation
Batch processing of simulation runs with minor parameter tweaks could be automated
Generating standard sections of reports (such as boilerplate content) is repetitive

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys conceptualizing sustainable design strategies and seeing simulated improvements
Finds early-stage design iterations with AI tools like SketchPro engaging
Considers repetitive data entry and report formatting tedious
Gets satisfaction from visualizing simulation results and identifying efficiency gains

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry and cleaning in Excel is time-consuming
Software incompatibility or file conversion issues slow down workflow
Running multiple simulation iterations can be slow and computationally intensive
Switching between different platforms (e.g., REVIT, OpenStudio, FUZOR) introduces friction

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive when working on tasks that align with personal interests in sustainability
Skill level is highest in areas of repeated practice, such as energy modeling and AutoCAD drafting
Enjoyment increases when learning leads to tangible project improvements
Productivity dips when faced with repetitive, low-skill tasks such as manual data entry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a positive and proactive attitude toward adopting new tech solutions
Sees technology as essential for improving workflow efficiency
Enjoys exploring how new tools can lead to better sustainability outcomes
Views tech learning as integral to staying current in the field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self adaptable and quick to pick up new platforms
Able to learn new simulation software within a short timeframe (1-2 weeks)
Comfortable experimenting with beta versions of emerging AI tools
Occasionally seeks guidance from more experienced peers during initial adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per week to learning new tools
Participates in online webinars and workshops when relevant
Prefers hands-on, project-based training over theoretical instruction
Balances training time with academic and project deadlines

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Primary motivation is increased efficiency and project impact
Relevance to current projects is a strong factor in prioritizing what to learn
High workload can occasionally delay tech adoption
Will avoid learning tools perceived as outdated or not widely used in industry

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often recommend new tools and share tips in group settings
Faculty advisors encourage the exploration of cutting-edge technologies
Collaborative culture promotes knowledge sharing and experimentation
Feedback from others helps in assessing tool effectiveness and relevance

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses REVIT for building modeling and documentation
Runs energy simulations in OpenStudio and Insight
Relies on Microsoft Excel for data management and reporting
Implements ALICE for generative construction schedule simulations
Utilizes FUZOR for 3D site logistics planning

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: AutoCAD is essential for 2D site mapping and drafting
QGIS is used for environmental and geospatial analysis
Excel is the primary tool for data entry, analysis, and visualization
SketchUp occasionally used for quick 3D conceptual modeling

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Comfortable with BIM platforms such as REVIT and AutoCAD
Has working knowledge of energy modeling software like OpenStudio and Insight
Familiar with 3D simulation tools (FUZOR) and geospatial platforms (QGIS)
Understands the basics of project management tools common in construction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces file compatibility issues between REVIT and OpenStudio
Data transfer between AutoCAD and QGIS requires manual adjustments
Integration of simulation outputs into Excel for reporting is sometimes cumbersome
Switching between platforms can cause version control and data loss concerns

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts such as machine learning, generative design, and automation
Familiar with terms like 'training data,' 'model inference,' and 'optimization algorithms'
Has attended introductory workshops or webinars on AI applications in construction
Can explain the difference between rule-based automation and AI-driven solutions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ALICE for generative simulation and construction scheduling
Employs OpenAI (e.g., ChatGPT) for meeting transcription and note organization
Utilizes SketchPro and Finch for AI-assisted conceptual design iterations
Leverages Excel’s AI-driven features for data organization and workflow optimization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses ALICE weekly for optimizing construction schedules in class projects
Occasionally employs OpenAI tools to transcribe and organize meeting notes
Applies Finch or SketchPro at the conceptual design stage for early visualizations
Uses Excel’s AI features when handling large datasets or automating repetitive formatting

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used ALICE to identify more efficient construction sequencing options, reducing project timelines in simulations
AI transcription tools occasionally misinterpret technical jargon, requiring manual editing
AI-assisted conceptual design tools accelerated early-stage iterations, improving team creativity
Initial attempts at automating energy model parameter sweeps with AI had mixed success due to data input inconsistencies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Receives informal peer-to-peer support for AI tool adoption
Occasional workshops provided by university or research group on emerging AI technologies
Online resources and forums are primary sources for troubleshooting
Faculty advisors sometimes recommend new AI tools or share best practices

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers a mix of independent exploration and step-by-step guidance when learning new AI tools
Often starts independently, then consults tutorials or peers for advanced features
Enjoys experimenting solo but values collaborative brainstorming during implementation
Adjusts interaction style based on complexity of the tool or task

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant or co-worker that enhances productivity
Leverages AI as an advisor during early design and simulation stages
Uses AI to handle repetitive or data-intensive tasks, freeing up time for creative work
Prefers AI systems that can suggest but not override human decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to integrating new AI-driven processes that improve efficiency
Willing to experiment with generative design AI in conceptual phases
Actively seeks out workflows where AI can automate or enhance routine tasks
Approaches highly automated workflows with cautious optimism, especially for critical tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and clarifying requirements
Uses command-based interfaces for precise, repeatable tasks
Values the ability to switch between conversational and structured input modes
Finds that conversational AI aids in learning and troubleshooting new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Optimizing building decarbonization requires deep understanding of sustainability principles
Site-specific context and climate data interpretation often depend on tacit expertise
Material selection for sustainability involves nuanced trade-offs best understood by specialists
AI tools are more effective when guided by human domain knowledge

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI to generate options and organize data, but reviews outputs manually
Relies on AI for initial suggestions, not for final decisions in critical design tasks
Confidence in AI increases with tool transparency and ability to validate results
Skeptical of AI outputs in cases with high context sensitivity or novel scenarios

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to automate repetitive data entry and simulation parameter sweeps
Wants human oversight for interpreting results and making design recommendations
Sees value in human-AI collaboration for complex problem-solving
Supports partial automation for reporting, with manual quality control

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for low-risk, routine tasks like data sorting or transcription
Finds full automation threatening when nuanced decision-making is required
Prefers to retain manual control or override options for simulation and design outputs
Supports high automation when AI outputs can be easily audited and validated

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and critical thinking skills
Technical proficiency in simulation software remains essential despite automation
Routine skills like data entry are becoming less important due to AI
Sees communication and interpretation skills as increasingly vital in an AI-rich workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to interpret and validate AI-generated outputs is a new responsibility
Developing prompt engineering skills for conversational AI tools
Becoming adept at integrating and troubleshooting new AI tools within existing workflows
Responsible for identifying opportunities to automate tasks with AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about automation of data-heavy, repetitive tasks
Not worried about AI replacing core sustainability expertise or creative design work
Views AI as a tool for augmentation rather than replacement in the current stage
Monitors industry developments regarding AI-driven job market shifts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manual review is standard practice for all AI-generated reports and designs
Can adjust or override AI-suggested schedules in ALICE before finalizing
Double-checks AI transcriptions for accuracy and context
Maintains backup copies of original data before applying AI-driven transformations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Research group increasingly encourages experimentation with AI tools
No formal policy, but growing awareness of AI’s role in sustainable design education
Peers are more open to adopting AI than faculty in some cases
Anticipates formal guidelines as AI tool use becomes more widespread

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will drive significant efficiency gains in the construction sector
Expects AI to accelerate progress toward sustainability and decarbonization goals
Recognizes potential for job displacement in highly repetitive roles
Sees AI as a catalyst for interdisciplinary collaboration and innovation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports ethical AI development with transparent decision-making processes
Concerned about bias in AI outputs, especially in design and planning
Believes human oversight is essential to ensure responsible AI use
Wary of overreliance on AI for critical decisions without validation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Wishes for AI tools that leverage historical design data for optimal building decarbonization strategies
Sees value in AI that can seamlessly integrate data across all modeling and simulation platforms
Desires more intuitive AI-driven design suggestion engines for early-stage projects
Would benefit from AI that can automatically flag and explain inconsistencies in simulation results

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine tasks to be almost fully automated, focusing human effort on creative and strategic aspects
Predicts increased collaboration with AI as a design co-creator, not just an assistant
Foresees need for advanced skills in AI validation and cross-disciplinary integration
Believes sustainability specialists will be more data-driven, leveraging AI for decision support

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop clear guidelines and best practices for AI tool use in projects
Invest in ongoing training and upskilling for AI literacy across teams
Encourage pilot projects and experimentation with emerging AI technologies
Promote an open culture for sharing AI successes and failures to accelerate learning
